story:
“Pour me a glass of poison!” he used to call as he came through the door, and like the good wife I was trained to be, I would run to serve him. Tonight I sit across from him, wondering if I’ve made the right choice, knowing it is too late to turn back. If I flee, he will follow. I will live forever looking over my shoulder, flinching at every blond man who passes.

The alcohol that once made him charming has become his weapon. It follows him home from business lunches and dinners, transforming him glass by glass—merry, then suspicious, then cruel. I used to skim past articles on abuse, thinking such misery could never reach a girl with a neat, middle-class life. That naïve girl is gone. I’ve walked barefoot over the shards of who I was, until even on my best days I forgot the shape of myself.

If there were red flags at the start, I doused them with the fire of my love. He wore masks so well—smiles for friends, good manners for family—that even I believed them, hating myself for being fooled. Everything had to be perfect: the house, the cars, the decor. Me. He told me I existed for him. By our first anniversary, my choices had thinned into his choices; by my thirtieth birthday, my autonomy was a memory I couldn’t clearly recall.

Then came the choice that unstitched the rest: he took from me what I cannot name without breaking. The hands that worked miracles on operating tables ended my miracle with blows. When I grieved, he laughed: “It would have ruined your figure.” Whatever hope I had left went silent. He would not show remorse. I was not brave enough to leave—fear kept me tethered—but I could no longer let him own the last piece of me.

I sit on the cold, beautiful couch I despise—the place he dragged me from to mete out punishment—trying to keep my face calm while the ground inside me shifts like colliding plates. He loosens his tie, lifts his glass. He asks why I skipped my shift at the store, already aware through Elizabeth—his extra set of eyes. I lie easily now; I tell him I had a migraine and needed my prescription. In truth, I drove to the cemetery, and afterward to the storage unit where I had hidden what I would need.

He tips his whiskey back. I watch his throat work. I think of the emptiness behind his handsome face, a mind like ice water or starless space. I do not tremble. I remind myself that I wanted no miracles, only myself. He reaches for his glass again, frowning as if the taste is slightly off, but by then he has already swallowed too much to matter.

When the poison takes hold, surprise flickers, then panic. The glass slips and shatters on the floor. His elegant fingers claw at his throat. Foam gathers at the corners of his mouth. “Wh-what have you done?” he gasps.

“Only what you asked,” I say. “I poured you a glass of poison.”

He shudders, exhales, and is still.

I have become the monster he made me.
